title: From Hugging Face to GitHub: Tracing License Drift in the Open-Source AI Ecosystem
summary: This paper conducts the first comprehensive audit of licenses for AI datasets and models on Hugging Face and their integration into GitHub projects. It reveals widespread non-compliance, with over a third of model-to-application transitions eliminating restrictive license clauses. The authors also develop a rule engine capable of detecting most license conflicts, providing crucial tools and data for automated compliance in open-source AI.
key_findings: 35.5% of model-to-application transitions eliminate restrictive license clauses by relicensing under more permissive terms, indicating systemic non-compliance.
The study performed the first end-to-end audit of licenses across 364 thousand datasets, 1.6 million models on Hugging Face, and 140 thousand GitHub projects.
A prototype rule engine was developed that can detect 86.4% of license conflicts in software applications.
License compliance is identified as a critical governance challenge in the open-source AI ecosystem.
methodology: The study conducted an end-to-end audit of licenses for datasets and models on Hugging Face and their downstream integration into open-source software applications on GitHub, complemented by the development of a rule engine for conflict detection.
bias_disclaimer: AI-generated analysis may contain inaccuracies or reflect biases from the underlying model. Always perform critical evaluation.

analysis.summary: This paper conducts the first comprehensive audit of licenses for AI datasets and models on Hugging Face and their integration into GitHub projects. It reveals widespread non-compliance, with over a third of model-to-application transitions eliminating restrictive license clauses. The authors also develop a rule engine capable of detecting most license conflicts, providing crucial tools and data for automated compliance in open-source AI.
key_insights: 35.5% of model-to-application transitions eliminate restrictive license clauses by relicensing under more permissive terms, indicating systemic non-compliance.
The study performed the first end-to-end audit of licenses across 364 thousand datasets, 1.6 million models on Hugging Face, and 140 thousand GitHub projects.
A prototype rule engine was developed that can detect 86.4% of license conflicts in software applications.
License compliance is identified as a critical governance challenge in the open-source AI ecosystem.
analysis.methodology: The study conducted an end-to-end audit of licenses for datasets and models on Hugging Face and their downstream integration into open-source software applications on GitHub, complemented by the development of a rule engine for conflict detection.
research_gaps: The rule engine, while effective, solves 86.4% of conflicts, indicating a remaining portion of undetected conflicts or limitations in its scope.

While identifying systemic non-compliance and providing detection tools, the paper does not delve into the underlying socio-technical causes of this non-compliance or propose specific policy interventions beyond detection.
